title.S: Chairman Mao's Red Braised Pork Belly

description.S: A legendary Hunan dish of slow-braised pork belly caramelized in sugar, perfumed with star anise and cinnamon, and finished with a glossy, savory-sweet sauce. The authentic Mao-style version features a touch of chili heat that distinguishes it from the sweeter Shanghai variant.

L.S: 2 pounds skin-on Pork Belly, cut into 1-inch cubes
5 Scallions, divided (2 for blanching, 3 for cooking - whites in 2-inch sections, greens thinly sliced)
1/2 cup plus 1 tablespoon Shaoxing Wine, divided
3 1/2 tablespoons Sugar, divided (or 40g Rock Sugar, crushed)

note.S: 2 for blanching; 3 for cooking - whites in 2-inch sections, greens thinly sliced

L.S: Place 2 pounds of pork belly cubes in a medium pot and cover with cold water by 1 inch. Add 3 ginger slices, 2 scallion sections, and 1 tablespoon Shaoxing wine. Bring to a boil over high heat, skimming the grey-brown foam from the surface with a fine-mesh skimmer. Reduce to a simmer and cook for 3-5 minutes until no more foam rises.

Drain the pork, optionally reserving the blanching liquid through a fine strainer. Discard the ginger and scallion. Rinse pork briefly under cold running water to wash off any clinging residue. Pat the pork thoroughly dry with paper towels to prevent splattering when added to hot caramelized sugar.

Heat 2 tablespoons oil and 3 tablespoons sugar in a heavy-bottomed Dutch oven or wok over low heat. Allow the sugar to melt slowly without stirring, swirling the pot gently instead. Watch for color changes from white to golden to light amber, about 3-5 minutes. Do not walk away as sugar goes from amber to burnt in seconds.

Add the dried pork pieces in a single layer the moment the sugar reaches a honey-amber color. Stand back slightly as there may be sputtering. Turn heat to medium and toss the pork to coat evenly in the caramel for 1-2 minutes, until the pork develops a rich reddish-brown sheen on all sides.

Push pork to one side of the pot. Add 3 remaining ginger slices, 3 smashed garlic cloves, white scallion sections, 2-4 dried red chili peppers, 1 cinnamon stick, 2 star anise, and 2 bay leaves. Stir-fry the aromatics for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Deglaze with 1/2 cup Shaoxing wine, scraping up any caramelized bits from the bottom of the pot. Let the wine bubble for 30 seconds to cook off the raw alcohol.

Add 3 tablespoons light soy sauce and 1 1/2 tablespoons dark soy sauce and stir to combine. Pour in 2-2 1/2 cups hot water or reserved blanching liquid until the liquid just barely covers the pork. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to low for a gentle simmer.

Cover with the lid slightly ajar, leaving a finger-width gap. Simmer for 1 to 1 1/2 hours, stirring gently every 20 minutes to prevent sticking. The pork is ready when it is very tender when pierced with a chopstick but still holds its shape, with translucent and jiggly fat and lean meat that yields easily.

Remove the lid if the sauce is still thin when the pork is tender. Increase heat to medium and reduce the sauce for 5-10 minutes, stirring frequently, until it becomes a thick, glossy glaze that coats the pork.

Taste the sauce and adjust by adding the remaining 1/2 teaspoon sugar if needed to balance any soy-sauce saltiness. Remove and discard the cinnamon stick, star anise, bay leaves, and large ginger slices.
Scatter the sliced green scallion parts over the top. Serve immediately over steamed white rice, accompanied by a light vegetable dish such as stir-fried bok choy or cucumber salad.
Gluten-Free Substitution: Replace light soy sauce and dark soy sauce with gluten-free tamari. Replace Shaoxing wine with dry sherry or mirin.
Dairy-Free: This recipe is naturally dairy-free as written.
Spice level: For a milder dish, use 1-2 chilies or omit. For authentic Hunan heat, use 4-6 dried chilies and consider adding 1/2 teaspoon of doubanjiang (chili bean paste) with the aromatics.
Rock sugar produces a cleaner, shinier caramel and is traditional. If using granulated white sugar, watch more carefully as it caramelizes faster.
The pork skin is essential to this dish. It becomes silky-gelatinous when braised and contributes rich body to the sauce. Do not remove it.
This dish improves overnight. Store in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 5 days, or freeze for up to 1 month. Reheat gently on the stovetop or steam until warmed through.
Additions: Hard-boiled eggs (add during the last 30 minutes), fried tofu puffs, or dried bamboo shoots can be braised alongside the pork.
